An FB post of [REDACTED_NAME]: CORNED BEEF These are the dead bodies of the 19 fatalities in Toboso, Negros Occidental, and people are commenting “corned beef” flooding with laugh reactions. I looked it up quickly and found that it’s being used as a derogatory slang term to mock the dead, implying they’ve been “ground up” or “pulverized” like canned meat during the clash. Utterly disgusting. This is a moral crisis. That is as bad as our educational and economic crises. Because this is the Philippines, a country that remains the largest Catholic nation in Asia and one of the largest in the world. Now, I welcome being called a sympathizer, if it only means I still have discernment. These are alleged members of the NPA, but what if they weren’t? Maybe a few of them were, but what about the rest? They had ammunition and guns, but what if those were planted? These are circumstantial proof, but what about forensic evidence? The thing is, if siding with the oppressed feels too uncomfortable, then at the very least—just be human. And being human is free.

I would like to sincerely reach out to this group that helps and supports jeepney drivers. I am a student of Public Administration coming from Tublay, and I’m humbly asking for your help for Tatay whose jeepney has the plate number AYG 158, traveling from Acop to Baguio. This morning, [REDACTED_NAME] 14, I rode his jeep. I handed him ₱50 and didn’t say that I was a student, but when he noticed my ID, he still chose to give me a discount. In the middle of this crisis, with fuel prices continuing to rise, he still thinks of others before himself. What truly broke my heart was seeing that his hand was wounded, yet he continued driving, continuing to work, just to make a living. Despite his situation, he still finds a way to be kind. People like him deserve to be seen and helped. He is not just a driver, he is someone's father and son. I am hoping that you can extend your kindness to him and include him in your Mission so that his jeepney, and other jeepneys coming from Tublay, may also have donation boxes.

Amidst the endless calls for the suspension of the fuel excise tax and an increase in transportation fare rates, I came across certain posts criticizing this initiative as unfair to commuters and laborers. Well, in fact, the drivers are not only affected by this oil price hike, but people from all aspects are “equally” affected. I even experienced a commuter (auntie) directly criticizing the call for these initiatives while riding a jeepney, “Buti nga sila may ayuda, papaano tayong commuters? Bakit parang sila lang yung may [REDACTED_NAME], sila lang yung affected, eh lahat naman tayo affected!” “THEY ARE CALLING FOR AN INCREASE IN FARE RATES, PERO YUNG SWELDO NG IBANG LABORERS WALANG INCREASE.” The jeepney driver felt speechless upon hearing her remarks. Though these frustrations and call-outs have a point and have some truth in them, it seems like these people are barking at the wrong tree. These calls for an increase in transportation rates and suspension of fuel excise tax are not giving the jeepney drivers an “advantage”. These drivers are not demanding too much, are not being entitled, and are not being exploitative - these calls are for their own survival, hanging by a thread. While we can assume that we are all affected equally by this global crisis and ongoing conflict, there is a saying, “we are in the same storm, but not on the same boat.” In contrast to laborers who have a stable income, whether it is minimum or not, the drivers depend on daily income from trips to sustain themselves and their families, which is sometimes a gamble, as fewer passengers mean less income; many of them do not even have their own vehicle to drive and are required to submit their own boundaries. Imagine a jeepney driver earning an average of PHP 5,000.00 to 7,000.00 daily - it may seem like a large sum of money, but if we take a look at the oil price as of today (PHP 155/ Liter), and a jeepney driver consumes an average of 30 liters per day (PHP 4,650.00), then 5,000.00 to 7,000.00 daily income, is subtracted by 4,650.00 for gas = PHP 350.00 to 2,350.00 daily income. Some drivers are often required to pay a boundary ranging from PHP 3,500.00 to PHP 4,000.00 per day, which renders their income negative. Given the current situation and rising prices of goods and services, the drivers are now completely crippled. To add, the family of these drivers also depends on this scanty income for their “bread and butter”. Do you still think it is unfair for the drivers to call for these initiatives? You should not call out the drivers who are struggling to survive. You should blame the higher-ups, who lack a concrete and feasible plan to address this global crisis. Let us not blame one another. - [REDACTED_NAME] ( hindi guni-guni) from the [DEPARTMENT]: [REDACTED_NAME] 11, 12:59:48 PM GMT

​I am requesting that this be posted immediately, [REDACTED_NAME] [REDACTED_NAME] much better, please, as the pressure we are currently facing has become incredibly difficult to manage. I would like to bring attention to the situation at Batangas State University Malvar, specifically regarding the Management Accounting program for fourth-year students. ​We would like to clarify why the NCIII Bookkeeping certification has been made a mandatory requirement for the Fourth Year Compre. Such certifications should remain a matter of personal choice for the students rather than a compulsory obligation. Furthermore, we are concerned as to why this accounts for 20% of our final grade. ​Given the current high costs of transportation and fuel, this adds a significant financial burden to our families. As graduating students, we are already managing numerous expenses, including graduation fees and pictorials. While we understand [REDACTED_NAME] "educational tour " sa program namin, it is unreasonable to replace it with a mandatory certification fee. ​It is disheartening that the students were never consulted nor surveyed regarding this examination; we were given no choice but to comply. The combination of financial strain and the academic pressure of our final comprehensive exams is becoming overwhelming. Most of us still rely on our parents for financial support, and these funds are not easily acquired. ​We urge the administration to show compassion and consider the welfare of the students in these decisions. Amidst the chaos that is happening right now including the one taking place in this page, I'd like to share about what I saw earlier. As I was commuting on a jeep, I saw someone giving her fare to the driver then gesturing to keep the change. I did not see how much she gave but no matter how much was it, it really touched my heart that even in this country full of perverse people, criminals in power and the rampant crimes in its every street - there are still people who choose to be kind. What I witnessed earlier was just one of many I saw of people being human, and everytime I see these deeds - it gives me hope and determination to be a better person that would later help change our country for better. IT'S STILL WORTH FIGHTING FOR WHATS RIGHT! For as long as there are people who choose to be kind, there is hope even in this desolate" country. To you who's reading this message, please choose to be kind for we are the future leaders of this country. Our predecessors may have done things that made things worse for us, but let us choose to be the change for the sake of the next generation. But how can that change happen if we quarrel over an out of tune guitar? Come on guys, we're better than this. This is a wake up call to everyone especially to us the youth that even when the system, has failed us - even when our own leaders cheat on us, let us choose to be kind. Who knows, a whole new generation of leaders with integrity and a strong desire for change may arise in our midst. May pag-asa pa.

CALL FOR PARTICIPANTS | [CAR] Queer Cordilleran Students Dearest participant, I am currently a graduate student pursuing a Master’s degree in Development Communication at [CAR]-Open University, and I am conducting a thesis titled: “Free to Fly the Rainbow Flag: Theorizing Queer-Inclusive Learning Environments at [CAR].” This study has been approved by the [CAR] Research Ethics Committee, and I am now inviting participants to be part of this meaningful research. Who can participate? • Must be a student of [CAR] • Must identify as part of the Queer/LGBTQIA+ community • Must be from the Cordillera region What is the study about? This research aims to understand: 1. How queer Cordilleran students conceptualize queer-inclusive learning environments 2. How queer and Indigenous identities shape experiences in university spaces 3. The challenges encountered in academic environments 4. How these challenges are negotiated 🧩 What will participation involve? The study will be conducted in several phases: • Participant selection • Information session • Photo generation (guided activity) • Participatory photo interpretation (PHOTO Model) • Data analysis • Exhibit showcasing participants’ insights Confidentiality Assured All information gathered will be treated with utmost confidentiality and will be used solely for academic research purposes. If you are interested in participating,, feel free to address the Google Form. GOOGLE FORM LINK: Let your voice be heard. Your story matters.

As a commuter, it also takes a toll, particularly for someone like me who travels from far away and needs to take two rides back and forth just to be able to attend. Not to mention, jeepneys in our area are also choosing not to operate because of this crisis. I can’t simply move closer to the school due to certain constraints. Let me share something about my perspective nowadays, so I'm the type of person who lack interest in things, nope, I'm not depressed or anything, it's just that I'm that type of person that's all. If your still readung this then feel free to read it until the very last statement. So, as I was saying, since I lack interest in things, I just do things out of duty but not out of interest, going to college for example. I don't really have plans after this, I just know that if I finished college then I could have a job and work. But lately, I want progress, I want change and I feel like I want to compete with myself for I feel that my life is so boring, rigid and I don't know. I like this version of myself better, a version who wants to see a better version of hers. It doesn't have to be the best, but I want to see progress. I'm still in the process but can I ask for tips on how to get out of my comfort zone and start changing my ways. Do note that I want progress, like PROGRESS, I want to be a person who can view myself in the future as someone who've done and choose to do greater heights. ty

I watched you from the sidelines of [REDACTED_NAME], rehearsing a confession in my head between every vitals sign check and every sleepless night of duty. I thought, maybe, a girl from the "Red School" could find a place in the world of the "Green." But tonight, the diagnosis came back, and it’s terminal. I finally did it. I searched for your name. I scrolled through your profile, my hand trembling as I hit "See More." And there she was. Not me.😭 The way you hold her the way your eyes light up in those photos is a harvest I’ll never get to share. You aren’t just the King of the Fields; you’re the king of her world, too. And here I am, standing in my crisp white uniform, feeling like a ghost in the hallways we both walk. I was ready to give you my time, my care, and my heart. But you’ve already planted your roots elsewhere.🫂 So, I’ll keep my stethoscope on and my head down. I’ll go back to the wards and the long shifts, pretending my pulse doesn't spike when I see you across the campus. Congratulations on the title, Mister CA. "Ilablab-ak mo" - a kankana-ey phrase which means "don't worry, everything will be aokay" But when you think about it, when will everything be okay? during our interview with farmers around here in Benguet, actually it wasn't just the data gathering that we needed that we did but also, we saw, heard, and felt the hardship, suffering, and sacrifice of every farmer🥹. imagine during the rainy days and this summer they are forced to work just to sell something for the needs of their families but why is that? even though their crops cost 3-5 pesos per kilo, they still think of working for the welfare of the people. And in that interview, most of the challenges that farmers face are very low wages, financial (especially if their income is low). All of those we interviewed said they don't receive anything from government agencies that could have helped them, it really makes me cry especially if you look them in the eye it's really heartbreaking💔🥹 this one said, if there is anything the government can give/help them, it doesn't reach them or they don't experience it because they are selective about who gets given and most especially those who have acquaintances/connections, why are those rotten habits still there!? How sad to think that while some are enjoying themselves, others are suffering. So please, whatever, whenever wherever we are, please show some respect to our beloved farmers because we don't know how important that is to them. And to the others out there who are criticizing agriculture, let's see someday if you don't repent "Always remember that without Agriculture you can't eat and you can't heal" #🌱🍀

I met her in the most unexpected way. That day, I was just wandering around during our intramurals, watching random games to pass the time because boredom had already settled in. Honestly, I was only there for attendance. Nothing more. I sat by the court and watched the girls’ basketball game, not really paying attention at first. I don’t even remember which teams were playing. The details faded the moment she caught my attention. And suddenly, the game was no longer just a game. There was something about her presence that pulled my eyes toward her. It was the kind of aura you notice without trying, the kind that makes the noise around you soften for a moment. I found myself watching her the entire time. Every movement she made on the court felt effortless. The way she ran, the way she handled the ball, the way she carried herself, it was all so natural, so cool, like the court was a place where she truly belonged. Without realizing it, I was already cheering for her, even though we weren’t from the same college. For a moment, I forgot why I was even there. I thought that would be the end of it when the game finished. Just a passing moment during an ordinary day. But then I saw her again. This time, she stood on the court not as a player, but as a referee, calm, composed, still carrying that same quiet strength. And somehow, that simple moment stayed with me longer than I expected. Yes, she’s from [DEPARTMENT]. Miss [REDACTED_NAME]. And without even knowing it, you caught my attention that day. To the BLIS students, please know that your efforts matter. Your support helped make the sportsfest possible, and your willingness to stand behind the scenes is something that deserves respect and appreciation. You may not always be the ones in the spotlight, but your contributions are a reminder that every successful event is built on the efforts of people who care enough to help.Even though many BLIS students did not participate in the sports competitions themselves, they played a vital role behind the scenes. From helping prepare props to assisting with different tasks that kept the event organized, their dedication and willingness to help did not go unnoticed. Events like this require teamwork, and BLIS showed that support does not only come from competing but also from contributing wherever help is needed. It is unfortunate that their efforts were sometimes overlooked or not fully appreciated by others within the (CIS). Support should always be recognized, especially when it comes from people who choose to help without expecting recognition. The BLIS community showed commitment, patience, and a genuine spirit of cooperation despite the discouragement they may have felt.

To the BSUkols in this page, The University Intramurals is supposed to be a celebration of sportsmanship, camaraderie, and unity (One [CAR], One Purpose: Sports for Excellence and Sustainability) not a battleground for college superiority. Yet here we are, watching this FW become a space where colleges are dragged and judgements are thrown around based purely on scores and rankings. Winning or losing in sports does not define the worth of a college and its students; it does not make one college superior, just as a lower ranking does not make another inferior. Every college in [CAR] exists for a purpose, and every student deserves basic respect regardless of how their team performed. What's more concerning is that this platform (meant to give students a safe and open space) is being used to tear people down under the comfort of anonymity. Anonymity is not a weapon. We are all part of one university. The moment we start ranking each other's dignity based on athletic results, we've already lost something far more important than any game. Let's do better, ka-[CAR]! (please admin, wag mo na pong i-allow na ma-post kung very hurtful na ang message) To our [CAR] profs, It concerns me the most when this kind of thinking comes from people in positions of authority. When a professor expresses disappointment by comparing colleges based on population and results, implying that losing is shameful, it stings in a way that's hard to ignore. We understand that you care, and that's appreciated ☺️. But there is a fine line between expressing disappointment and making students feel like they are not enough. Our worth as students, and the value of our courses, does not begin and end on a playing field. What we study, what we contribute, and who we are matters far beyond any scoreboard. Now that UI is wrapping up I just wanted to give my appreciation for all the teaching and non-teaching staff who joined. I watched the basketball women’s game between the faculty and my college. Of course I was rooting for my college but honestly the officiating was pretty frustrating. There were so many unfair calls from the refs and the faculty definitely didn’t deserve that kind of calls. You could really tell by the way they moved that most of them were probably student-athletes back in the day. It was impressive to see the teachers keeping up with the younger players. I really hope that for the next UI the officiating is more balanced. Everyone is out there playing their hearts out, but bad calls really ruin the spirit of fair play.

Friendly reminder to my fellow schoolmates, classmates, etc... The freedom wall was created so students could share thoughts, raise concerns, discuss issues, and exchange ideas about campus life. It was not created as a training ground for people who think “freedom of speech” means posting shallow insults, misinformation, or low-effort complaints with zero understanding of the situation. Freedom of expression actually assumes something very radical: that the person speaking has at least attempted to think first. Shocking concept, I know. The point of a Freedom Wall is dialogue, awareness, and constructive criticism that can help improve the university environment. When it’s reduced to baseless rants and attention-seeking posts, it stops being a space for discussion and starts looking more like a public display of poor critical thinking. So yes, everyone is free to post. But if the goal is to prove you can speak without understanding what you’re talking about, congratulations—you’re using the Freedom Wall perfectly... just not the way it was originally intended.

CNAS students spend years studying, doing research, passing difficult courses, and building real skills in their fields. Meanwhile, the only contribution you made to the conversation was a paragraph full of negativity and caps lock. And talking about instructors that way? That just shows a lack of respect. You don’t see the amount of effort, preparation, and dedication they put into teaching and guiding students every single day. It’s easy to throw insults online, but it’s a different story when it comes to actually contributing something meaningful. At the end of the day, if there’s anything embarrassing here, it’s not CNAS. What’s embarrassing is the mindset behind a post like this—judging an entire college while presenting an argument that lacks both substance and maturity. Next time, try bringing actual constructive criticism instead of bitterness and insults. Because right now, the post says a lot more about your character than it does about CNAS. - E

APPRECIATION POST SUBJECT: To know and personally thank the Ate Nursing Student who didn't hesitate to help run me to [CAR] clinic from Wangal On March 11, (Wednesday) around 2 PM, right after the 1st heat of Men's 4x100m relay at Wangal (Benguet Sports Complex), I was immediately run to [CAR] clinic. Before I got to participate in the said category, I was feeling a bit unwell but brushed it off, thinking that my stomach was just bloated, but turned out to be my Acid-related Disorder striking up again. After I ran my part for the 4x100m, my stomach suddenly shrunk and got hard, so I had to lay down for a bit --- it was cramps in response to my hyped activity during the flare-up of my condition, as per the doctor's analysis. Fortunately, my friends [REDACTED_NAME] and [REDACTED_NAME] were there to help. They were running me to [CAR] clinic as I couldn't manage to move my body. Right at the entrance of the Sports Complex, two nursing students ([REDACTED_NAME] and [REDACTED_NAME], I think their names were) were there. One of them stood up and helped me walk to the highway. And when we were there, no jeep seemed to be taking my side, [REDACTED_NAME] HAHAHAH. I can’t believe professors sometimes act on emotions and immediately decide to load students with additional activities. When emotions run high, sometimes extra activities get added even if the situation causing the frustration is something students can’t really control. Sometimes this is even delivered in a sarcastic tone. With intramurals happening at the same time as the announcement of the 4-day workweek for fuel conservation, schedules are already tight. Despite the memo that no classes should be conducted, we are left with no choice but to have classes since laboratory remedials might not be possible once the possible 7 AM–7 PM (Monday-Thursday) schedule starts, even when we’re supposed to be participating in intramurals and not focused on studying. I just hope that decisions affecting students are made with a bit more consideration for timing and circumstances.

Well, that person happens to be a male intern in the admin VP office. Funny enough, many of my colleagues even thought he was already a faculty member because of how professional he sounds when talking to others. Very composed, respectful, and confident. But what people probably notice the most is the light energy he carries. There’s something about his presence that makes people feel more at ease. Conversations become less tense, and the mood somehow softens. And when he smiles, it’s the kind of smile that naturally makes people relax too. Nothing too loud, nothing too obvious, just a quiet kind of presence that people around the office have started to admire. Heard him being called "Sander pareceive naman ito".

NOT a hate post If you feel gay, then go. Do whatever you want to do. Dress up, make up, I don't care. But what I do care about is when you go to women's bathroom stalls. That's a different thing. If you feel gay, you are free to feel whatever you want, but do not think that makes everything else okay. I do not feel comfortable when I see gays in a woman's/female bathroom. You have your rights as gay but where are our rights to feel safe as women? Especially that this month is women's month. There's always talk about safety and rights, but my rights to feel safe is not met. I don't remember seeing any women who claim to be men going to the men's bathroom. Why? Because they are still women and feel much safer in a woman's bathroom. This is not to belittle your feelings, but you have to set boundaries and care about what we feel, too. Although what you feel about yourself is your right, having everyone else go along with it is not. - From a concerned student

This message is not meant to come from a place of disrespect, but simply to share the perspective of some students who are still adjusting to the pace and expectations of college life. We recognize the effort that goes into organizing activities and managing events. We understand that these responsibilities are not easy, and we appreciate the time and energy seniors put into making these things possible for the college community. We also understand that many of the systems being followed today were already practiced by the batches before us, and that our seniors are simply continuing them with some adjustments. At the same time, we hope our concerns can also be heard. There are situations where students who are not directly participating in activities are still required to stay for long hours mainly for sign-in and sign-out attendance. We should be able to enjoy these kinds of events without the worry of paying the penalty or constantly checking the alloted time for attendance. For some students, this can be challenging as mentioned to the previous entry here in the freedom wall. Another concern is when plans change during the day, particularly regarding attendance. When the location for sign-in or sign-out changes, it can be difficult for students to know where to go. Some end up spending time just trying to find where the attendance is being held. For students who commute or manage tight schedules, these sudden changes can affect their time and movement. Perhaps having one clear and consistent attendance area, announced ahead of time, could help reduce confusion. This message is not meant to avoid participation or responsibility. Many of us genuinely want to support events and be part of the college community. We simply hope that through clearer communication, consistent arrangements, and mutual understanding between batches, participation can become more meaningful and manageable for everyone. At the end of the day, us, students share the same space and community. Hopefully, by listening to each other’s perspectives, we can make things better for everyone. 😊

Dear [CAR] Administration: This is in reference to Memorandum Circular No. 114, s. , issued by the Office of the President, which directs all government agencies to strictly adopt energy conservation protocols and implement a four-day on-site work arrangement as mandated by President [REDACTED_NAME], Jr. As no internal memorandum or official directive has yet been issued regarding this matter (BY THE UNIVERSITY), we respectfully raise the following concerns for your consideration: FIRST: we hope that faculty members will not be required to work from home on Fridays, as some classes may be conducted online. It is important to note that not all students have reliable internet access at home, nor can all afford to purchase data to attend virtual classes. We kindly emphasize that not all [CAR] students come from financially privileged backgrounds. SECOND: should the proposed 8:00 AM to 7:00 PM work schedule be adopted, we respectfully request that measures be taken to ensure punctuality among employees. Even under the current 8:00 AM to 5:00 PM schedule, some staff members report as late as 9:00 or 10:00 AM, which causes delays and inconvenience. Please be reminded that [CAR] offices and buildings are not located in close proximity to one another. For instance, if one needs to walk from a building at the far end of the campus to the administration office, only to find that the staff member concerned is not yet present, it results in unnecessary waiting and disruption. We are aware of circulating rumors regarding financial issues and "maraming utang na di pa bayad". We wish to emphasize that these allegations do not pertain to the current Mr. [REDACTED_NAME]. To our dear seniors, We understand your message, and we acknowledge the hard work and sacrifices you’ve made. We respect that you went through difficult first-year experiences and still showed up. However, we also hope our side can be understood. Many first years are not refusing to show up out of unwillingness. The issue for some of us is how participation is being handled. For example, requiring students who are not participants to stay only for sign-in and sign-out attendance can feel unnecessary. Instead of spending hours waiting with nothing to contribute, some students could have used that time to rest, study, or recover from the workload we are currently facing. Burnout is not always an excuse. For many students, it is a real struggle while adjusting to college life, academic pressure, and new responsibilities. Being present physically does not always mean someone is able to give their best mentally. We respect the effort seniors put into organizing activities, and we appreciate those opportunities. At the same time, we hope there can also be consideration for the situation of first years who are still learning how to manage everything. Maybe instead of interpreting absence as unwillingness, we can find better ways to encourage participation without forcing students to stay when they are not involved. We are not trying to avoid responsibility. We are simply asking for understanding, balance, and better communication between batches. Respect should go both ways, and we believe that with that, both seniors and first years can support each other better. 🩺

A friendly reminder and criticism to my fellow college mates sa CIS, especially to THAT specific circle na BSIT freshmen. I just want to ask? have you ever heard of Netiquette or any information kung paano rumespeto [REDACTED_NAME] privacy? If not, the internet is free, try to take a little time to read about it and reflect. Most of us were already introduced to this back in high school, so it shouldn’t be something completely new. One basic example of it is respecting other people’s privacy and dignity online and offline. Taking a picture of someone without their consent and then sharing it with friends just to make fun of it is not okay it will never be. What’s even more disappointing is that wagas kung makatawa while imitating the person’s expression, na Para bang comedy show. Sometimes we forget that the person in that picture is also someone with feelings. There is a saying that goes, “Tell me who your friends are, and I will tell you who you are.” Our circle influences our behavior more than we realize. [REDACTED_NAME], in situations like that, may [REDACTED_NAME] na magsabi na mali iyon. Even a simple “Guys, that’s not right” can already change the direction of the situation. Maybe some people actually disagreed but stayed silent because it’s easier to go along with the group. But silence can sometimes make the wrong thing look acceptable. If you ever find yourself in a circle where making fun of others is normal, maybe it’s also worth asking yourself if that environment is really helping you grow. Over time, the habits of the people around us slowly become our own habits too. That’s why it’s important to choose friends who also encourage respect and good character, kahit man [REDACTED_NAME] good pero [REDACTED_NAME] iakma niyo [REDACTED_NAME], Or [REDACTED_NAME] din alam I differentiate ang [REDACTED_NAME]? I don't mean na hindi na tayo pwedeng magbiro o tumawa, but there’s a difference between joking with someone and laughing at someone. At the end of the day, we are students in the field of technology. That means we should be among the first to understand digital responsibility, respect, and proper behavior online. Knowledge in technology should go hand in hand with good character. [REDACTED_NAME] we try to be examples not just in skills, but also in how we treat people. A small act of respect will not kill and degrade you as person but uplift your character. Sorry, nainis [REDACTED_NAME] in the sight of that. To our dear first years 🩺 We've heard "too busy", "too tired" and especially "BURNOUT" more times than we can count. And honestly? We're not here to dismiss that. We were first years once too. We know the sleepless nights, the overwhelming requirements, the feeling na halos hindi ka na makakain sa dami ng kailangan mong gawin. That experience is real, and we see you. But here's the thing — dumaan [REDACTED_NAME] [REDACTED_NAME], and we still showed up. Meanwhile, your seniors are juggling hospital duties, community health work, and actual clinical responsibilities with real patients and real stakes — and we are still showing up. And before you say na "choice [REDACTED_NAME], iba kami" ehh [REDACTED_NAME], choice [REDACTED_NAME]. Just like it's your choice now. But choices have a way of reflecting your character as a student. And right now, the choice to CONSISTENTLY OPT OUT says more than you might intend it to. Not too long ago, you were vocal na our activities were not "exciting" enough. Now here comes the University Intramurals and somehow, yung energy is STILL nowhere to be found. The same reasons are being recycled. We're not asking na maging sunod sunuran kayo sa mga seniors. We're not asking you to show up with a smile if you're exhausted. We're asking you to try because the habit of showing up, [REDACTED_NAME], is exactly the kind of person this path will require you to be. So which is it — not enough exciting activities, or not enough willingness to show up for them? We believe you're capable of more than the EXCUSES. We were you once, and kaya namin. [REDACTED_NAME]. The list of concerns regarding your batch keeps on growing and hindi [REDACTED_NAME]. The alumnis are watching too. Maybe it's time to look at yourselves honestly and ask what you really want to be remembered as. We hope your goal as 1st years isn't just to reach that big event kung saan you will be able to finally and proudly wear your uniforms and caps. We hope you're here to actually become someone worth looking up to since you won't be first years forever. One day you'll be the seniors, and the batch after you will be watching.

Happy Women’s Month! Women empowerment is not just about being strong, loud, or “independent.” It’s also about character—how you treat other women when you have the chance to either protect them or hurt them. If you know a man is taken, the most dignified thing you can do is walk away. Not because you’re weak, but because you respect yourself enough to not accept leftovers, secret love, or attention that comes from betrayal. A man who cheats for you will eventually cheat on you. And the “validation” you get today becomes anxiety tomorrow—because deep down, you’ll always wonder if he’ll do the same to you. Women should respect other women. We don’t need to compete for a man’s attention, especially not a man who cannot be loyal. There is nothing empowering about being part of someone else’s pain. It’s not “winning.” It’s choosing temporary excitement over long-term peace and dignity. All women is a queen: a real queen is not crowned by beauty alone. Beauty gets attention, yes—but values keep respect. A queen should have class, discipline, and a clean heart. She should know how to carry herself with dignity, even when temptation is right in front of her. A queen doesn’t take pride in being chosen in secret. She doesn’t need to prove she can “steal” someone—because she knows her worth doesn’t come from another woman’s loss. A queen has boundaries. She chooses what is right even when it’s hard, even when nobody is watching. She chooses love that is honest, safe, and respectful—not love that requires lying, hiding, and hurting. Because real confidence isn’t about being desired; it’s about being honorable. Real empowerment is being able to say: “I won’t be the reason another woman cries.”�Real strength is walking away from situations that damage your soul.�And real self-respect is refusing to settle for anything that makes you smaller inside. Protect your dignity. Choose integrity. Choose peace. Because in the end, your reputation, your heart, and your future are worth more than stolen moments.

Replying to Are you 100% sure they were laughing at the child, or did you just assume that and run straight to Freedom Wall? That’s a pretty heavy accusation to throw at “three nursing students in uniform” when your entire post is based on what you THINK happened. Advocating for a child with autism is a good thing, no one is arguing with that. But real advocacy also means being responsible with what you say and post. Publicly calling people out without actually confirming the situation doesn’t make you righteous. it just spreads accusations that may not even be true. You’re asking them how they would feel if they or someone they loved had autism. Fair question. But maybe also ask yourself: how would you feel if someone posted about you online accusing you of something embarrassing or disrespectful without even knowing the full story? Freedom Wall shouldn’t be a place where people just post assumptions and turn them into public shame. If you really cared about addressing the situation, you could’ve approached them right there and clarified what was happening. Instead, you chose to post an accusation online and let people judge them based on your perspective alone. Empathy shouldn’t only apply to the narrative you created, it should apply to everyone involved. Because right now, what you posted isn’t awareness, it’s speculation. Big words coming from someone brave enough to accuse others, but only anonymously on the Freedom Wall. College of 👩‍⚕️

JEEPNEY ETIQUETTE !!️ As a fellow [CAR] student, I’ve noticed how some of us behave in public transportations. They are loud, inconsiderate, and unaware of seats designated for the elderly, pregnant women, and persons with disabilities. I sincerely hope we can practice courtesy and respect wherever we go, not just inside the campus. I’ve been riding jeepneys for years, and it’s saddening and frustrating to witness situations that could easily be avoided with simple consideration. Sometimes, people have to say “Excuse me” just to find a seat. There are instances where elderly passengers, already weak, are forced to move toward the middle because the seats for them are occupied and the person refuses to adjust. I’ve even heard comments like, “Ay apo dagitoy nga studyante,” which reflects poorly on us as a community. There are also passengers who refuse to pass the fare, or when they do, they hand it to someone who still has to move far just to reach the driver. And perhaps the worst are groups who speak loudly and laugh excessively, even when seated close together, disregarding the comfort of others. This is for awareness and a gentle reminder for all of us to please keep your voices down, especially when riding in groups. Some passengers may be exhausted from a long day and simply want a quiet ride home. Sit properly and adjust when needed to conserve space. Move closer together to make room for new passengers. Offer assistance in passing fare. Most importantly, prioritize seats near the entrance for the elderly, pregnant women, and persons with disabilities. Good manners should not disappear once we leave the school grounds. Let’s represent [CAR] with respect, empathy, and social awareness even in something as simple as a jeepney ride. Iyaman! 🙌

to all the students here constantly whining about “so much workload” or “too many tasks”—let me spell it out for you: just because you’re a first-year college student doesn’t mean the world owes you a free ride. If you have the time to complain here, type up your rants, and scroll through posts, congratulations—you also have the time to open your books, review your lessons, and actually do your assignments. And for those whining that teachers or professors have “no consideration” because you’re new to college—newsflash: nobody cares. Being a freshman isn’t a get-out-of-responsibility card. Your professors aren’t here to coddle you, they’re here to teach you. Consideration doesn’t mean lowering standards, it means learning to manage your time, prioritize, and grow up. So stop pretending the universe is out to get you. Stop wasting your own time and everyone else’s sympathy. Use the energy you spend whining to actually do something productive for once. Honestly, if effort matched your complaints, you’d be top of the class already—but apparently, that’s too much to ask. [REDACTED_NAME] addan [REDACTED_NAME] [REDACTED_NAME] "freedom wall to, we can say whatever we want", Yes, this is a freedom wall, and everyone is free to express their thoughts. However, freedom of expression does not mean freedom from accountability or criticism. Just as you are free to post complaints, others are equally free to respond and disagree. Having a platform to speak does not automatically make every complaint productive or reasonable. College is meant to develop not only knowledge but also responsibility, resilience, and professionalism. If we normalize constant public complaints instead of constructive solutions, we are not using that freedom wisely. Freedom allows you to speak. Maturity determines how you use it.

Thai stall, let’s drop the act. You approach students first. Hindi [REDACTED_NAME] — kayo ang nanghaharang. The moment someone says “no,” may side comment agad about their eyebrows. “[REDACTED_NAME], ayusin natin ‘yan.”So mang-iinsulto muna bago mag-offer ng “libre”? Then the magic line: “Libre lang, promise.” Paulit-ulit hanggang mapilitan yung tao umupo kasi nakakahiya na tumanggi. That’s not hospitality. That’s pressure. One of my friends was just roaming the fair. [REDACTED_NAME]. Nilapitan, kinulit, sinabihang libre. So she sat down — because libre nga daw. After [REDACTED_NAME], biglang may product presentation. Biglang may ₱200 eyebrow pencil na “kailangan.” When she said she had no cash (she only carries cards), may scoff pa. “[REDACTED_NAME].” Imagine accusing a student of lying just because [REDACTED_NAME]. Hindi pa natapos doon. [REDACTED_NAME]. Online banking. Inofferan pa siyang samahan mag-withdraw. Samahan magwidraw?For an eyebrow pencil! That’s not normal sales behavior — that’s cornering someone until they give in just to leave. Out of discomfort and pure pressure, she borrowed ₱200 just to get out of that situation peacefully. ₱200 for a product that doesn’t even live up to the “long-lasting” and “waterproof” claims. And here’s the worst part — when people hesitate, may dagdag pa na linyang "support niyo na, LGBTQ business to.” Let’s be very clear. Being part of the LGBTQ community does NOT excuse manipulative selling tactics. Advocacy is not a marketing shield. Identity is not a free pass for unethical behavior. Support is supposed to be voluntary — not extracted through guilt, shame, and social pressure. When “support us” becomes a tool to silence discomfort or force a sale, that’s exploitation — not empowerment. [REDACTED_NAME] may nag-complain, may troll account pa na body-shaming instead of addressing the issue. So the response to valid criticism is insults? That says more about your professionalism than any review ever could. If your product was worth it, you wouldn’t need: – to insult someone’s appearance – to trap them in a chair – to accuse them of lying – to pressure digital payments – to weaponize “support LGBTQ” A good business earns support. It doesn’t demand it. It definitely doesn’t manipulate it. Students went to the fair to enjoy — not to be cornered, shamed, and guilt-tripped into buying something they never asked for. Libre should mean free. No should mean no. And support should never feel like obligation. If this is your strategy, don’t act surprised when people start warning others instead of recommending you.

I did not realize how much it was affecting me until I found myself close to tears. It did not happen all at once. It started with small things. My leadership being questioned. My decisions being doubted. My character quietly judged. I tried to understand, to adjust, to listen. But deep inside, I knew I was only doing what I believed was right. There were moments when I had to step in because no one else would. I spoke up when something felt wrong. I chose responsibility over comfort. And somehow, that made me the difficult one. The bad one. The problem. Then came the distance. Separate group chats. Separate circles. Conversations that changed when I entered the room. Laughter that felt unfamiliar. Whispers that were never meant for me to hear, yet loud enough for me to feel. I slowly became an outsider in a place where I once felt I belonged. I kept telling myself to stay strong. To be professional. To rise above it. But carrying everything alone is exhausting. Fighting battles quietly takes a toll. And one day, it all felt too heavy. I was tired of proving myself. Tired of standing alone. Tired of feeling unseen. That was the day I wanted to cry. Then I came across a post that spoke directly to my heart. It reminded me that being misunderstood does not mean I am wrong. That standing alone does not mean I am weak. That sometimes doing the right thing comes with a cost. I still feel the weight of it all. But I also feel something else now. A quiet strength. A reminder that I know my intentions. I know my heart. And that has to be enough. Priya

Hi! I hope no one has to be the outcast of the group! A few weeks ago, I felt my COF started to avoid me, so in the first few days, they reduced communication, which is fine by me because I'm not the type of person who is extroverted. I am an ambivert, and it really depends on whether I have the energy to match theirs or if I am always tired and want to go home. And then as the day went by, they started hanging out without me, and I felt they were avoiding me, so I just went to my subjects alone or sat by myself. And I just realized that I hope I didn't pass the exam, and that I followed my friends who went to private school, and that I won't feel suffocated every time I attend my classes. I wish I had shifted my course earlier; perhaps I wouldn't feel this way. And some may say, "[REDACTED_NAME] nagustuhan or [REDACTED_NAME] sinabing [REDACTED_NAME]", I am always aware of what to say and my actions. So yeah! I don't know who to tell about this experience. I hope to every group of friends out there: try to communicate, like asking how are they, with all your friends and don't exclude anyone, because it can also affect that person's mental state. Thank you -From college of ___ (___ year na nais nang lumipat ngunit nasasayangan sa oras at pagod na nilaan para lamang pumasa😩)

White Coats and Heavy Crowns: A Personal and Professional Reflection Recently, I came across photos of clinicians wearing their white coats inside a bar — smiling, celebrating, and clearly enjoying themselves. Let me be clear: I have nothing against celebration or camaraderie. I was once a veterinary student myself, and I understand the culture of bonding after exhausting exams, toxic duties, and emotionally draining cases. We all deserve moments of relief and joy. But when I saw those images, I felt disappointed. Not because they were having fun — but because of where and how they chose to represent themselves. I could not help but ask: Was it necessary to wear the white coat there? Was it worth the possible misunderstanding? As professionals, our actions do not exist in isolation. Even when something feels harmless, it can influence how others perceive our profession. The white coat is not an accessory. It is not fashion. It is not simply a uniform. It is a symbol — of trust, competence, sacrifice, and responsibility. When pet owners entrust us with the lives of their beloved animals, they see more than a person; they see the profession behind that coat. When we wear it, we carry that representation with us. Professionalism does not end when clinic hours are over. In today’s digital world, someone is always watching. A single photo can travel farther than we intend. Especially for seniors and clinicians, there is an added weight of responsibility. Leadership is not only shown in surgical skills or diagnostic knowledge, but also in conduct and discernment. As the saying goes, “With great privilege comes great responsibility.” The privilege of wearing that coat demands that we protect its dignity. Being proud of our profession is honorable. But pride without mindfulness can easily cross into carelessness. The white coat should elevate the profession, not unintentionally downgrade it. There is nothing wrong with celebrating achievements, building friendships, or enjoying a night out. However, there is wisdom in knowing that some symbols belong in certain spaces. This reflection is not written to condemn, but to remind. All freedom has its limits. All celebrations have boundaries. As future doctors and current professionals, we must remember that we are always ambassadors of the vocation we chose. Respect for our uniform is respect for our patients, our clients, our alma mater, and the generations who will follow after us. We can celebrate. We can have fun. We can live fully. But let us do so responsibly. Let the clinic be where the white coat shines. Let our professionalism remain intact even outside hospital walls. Because once we step into this calling, we carry it everywhere we go. And that responsibility never comes off. #

I've been sein a lot of post here at the freedom wall that chk students daw are mabanton, naangot. We’re sorry if we sometimes smell, but please understand that it’s never because we don’t care about ourselves it’s because we give everything we have. Most of us [DEPARTMENT] students are athletes, and every drop of sweat, every ache, every exhausted breath is proof of the hard work and sacrifice we pour into our training every single day. We wake up before the sun rises, push our bodies beyond pain, run drills under the scorching heat, endure bruises and sore muscles, and often go straight from practice to class without a moment to rest. Yet, all people see is the smell not the early mornings we wake up for, not the late nights we spend practicing, not the discipline, dedication, and heart that we carry for our team, our school, and ourselves. It’s not laziness. It’s not carelessness. That odor is a sign of commitment, determination, and the effort to be better every day, even when it’s uncomfortable, even when it’s hard. We are humans who sweat, who strive, who fight not just for trophies or medals, but to push ourselves, to grow, and to honor the trust and expectations placed upon us. So before judging us for a smell, please see the sweat behind it, the battles we fight silently, and the heart we put into every step. We ask not for pity, but for understanding, respect, and a little empathy because being a [DEPARTMENT] athlete isn’t easy, and we’re doing our best, day in and day out. - 1A

What a SHAME for the college of P!nk. We know that laboratory exam week is important, and we are doing our best to focus and pass our subjects. But it really hurts that our Sports Fest was taken away so easily. Sports Fest is not just a game for us—it is something we look forward to, something that helps us breathe, feel excited, and find strength to keep going to school despite the pressure and stress. What hurts more is the unfairness of it all. The “tryouts” are not enjoyable because only a few are chosen, leaving many students with no chance to play, no chance to be part of the sports league. Those same chosen students will represent the university in intramurals, yet we do not even get our own Sports Fest. Every other college department already experienced theirs, while our department was left behind because of slow action from those in power. We waited, we hoped, and we stayed quiet but nothing happened. Shame on you for forgetting the students who only want fairness, inclusion, and a moment of joy in their college life. We are more than just exam scores we are human too. We still hope that the Sports Fest will push through. Despite everything, we believe that listening to students and giving us this opportunity will remind everyone that college life should have balance, fairness, and moments of joy. -

“Who says Forestry is an easy course?” Who says it’s only for students who are “not so smart,” who ran out of options, or chose it as a last resort? **Think again.** 🌲 **Forestry is a SCIENCE.** And not just one science. It is **Biology and Ecology.** It is **Math, Statistics, and Research.** It is **Law, Policy, and Governance.** It is **Art, Management, Economics, and Entrepreneurship.** It is **Community Development and Conservation.** Forestry is **not** just about planting trees. And it is **definitely not for the weak** not in **mind, body, heart, or spirit.** Because in Forestry, you learn things like: 1️⃣ **Forest Ecology** You study biological and abiotic interactions within forest ecosystems—how plants, animals, soil, water, and climate interact—and how to assess, document, and analyze biodiversity (both flora and fauna). 2️⃣ **Dendrology** You familiarize yourself with **200–300 forest tree species**, learning their **common names, scientific names, family names**, and **conservation status** no shortcuts. 3️⃣ **Wood Identification** You identify wood species based on **pore structure, grain patterns, texture, and color**. (Yes, woods have fingerprints.) 4️⃣ **Plant Physiology, Anatomy, and Morphology** You dive into how plants function and survive—**photosynthesis (Calvin Cycle), respiration, growth hormones, tissues, and structures.** And that’s just the beginning. 📚 **Other major Forestry subjects include:** 🌊 **Watershed Management** – Managing land and water resources to prevent erosion, floods, and water scarcity. 🦌 **Wildlife Conservation** – Protecting wildlife species and their habitats while balancing human needs. 🌾 **Range Management** – Sustainable management of grasslands and grazing ecosystems. 💧 **Hydrology** – Understanding water movement, availability, and quality in forested landscapes. 🌦 **Meteorology** – Studying weather and climate and how they affect forests and ecosystems. 🌱 **Forest Soils** – Soil formation, fertility, and nutrient cycles essential for forest growth. ⚖️ **Forest Policies and Governance** – Laws, regulations, and institutions that manage forest resources. 👥 **Forestry Extension** – Working with communities to apply forestry knowledge on the ground. 💰 **Forest Economics** – Valuing forest resources, cost–benefit analysis, and sustainable utilization. 🪵 **Wood Physics and Mechanics** – Understanding wood strength, structure, and behavior under stress. 🐝 **Beekeeping (Apiculture)** – Pollination ecology and forest-based livelihood systems. 🗺 **Geographic Information Systems (GIS)** – Mapping, spatial analysis, and data-driven forest planning. 📐 **Surveying** – Measuring land, boundaries, and forest areas accurately. 📏 **Forest Mensuration and Statistics** – Measuring trees, stands, and forest productivity using math and statistics. 🏭 **Forest-Based Industries** – Processing and value addition of forest products. 🌿 **Wood and Non-Wood Forest Products** – Timber, resins, medicinal plants, fibers, and more. 📊 **Forest Statistics and Informatics** – Data analysis, modeling, and decision support systems. 🌰 **Seed Identification** – Recognizing forest seeds for conservation and propagation. 🌱 **Nursery and Silvicultural Practices** – Growing, managing, and regenerating forests sustainably. 🔥 **Forest Fire Management** – Fire behavior, prevention, and control strategies. 🐜 **Forest Entomology** – Insects that help—or destroy—forest ecosystems. 🦠 **Forest Pathology** – Tree diseases and their management. 🌾 Agroforestry – Integrating trees with crops and livestock for sustainable food production, soil conservation, and climate resilience. 👥 Social Forestry – Empowering communities to manage forest resources for livelihoods, equity, and sustainability. 🌍 Climate Change Science – Understanding carbon cycles, climate modeling, mitigation, adaptation, and the role of forests as carbon sinks. 🌳 **Forestry shapes scientists, managers, researchers, policymakers, entrepreneurs, and conservation leaders.** It trains you to think critically, work physically, respect nature, and serve communities. So the next time someone says, **“Ay forestry lang dayta.”** 👉 **Think again.** student [REDACTED_NAME], ano daw pinag-aaralan sa Forestry 😁

"Ah, psychology. [REDACTED_NAME]." Then, he laughed with his friend. Those were the exact words I heard after my class. Imagine hearing some words of uplifts from your instructor about how important mental health was then you'd just hear these words outside. I understand that some people might have little knowledge about psychology but laughing about a difficult circumstance that you yourself would never want to have shows that you are yet to meet the event in your life that would humble you. “Bagtit” is not the right term for that. Mental illness matters, mental health matters. And you don't just laugh about a word that means a thousand stabs to other people. Please, let's be wary of what we say, of what we laugh about, and of what might hurt others. If you never heard of any topics regarding mental health, here's for you; Mental health • As much as I want to stop and explain some important things about our course, I had a class to attend. But, take note: Psychology is the "scientific study" of the mind and behavior, according to the American Psychological Association. Psychology is a multifaceted discipline and includes many sub-fields of study such as human development, sports, health, clinical, social behavior and cognitive processes (Mcleod, ). ​Reference: Mcleod, S. However, I hope we can also reflect on how and where these issues are being shared. As a "deemed" professional college, it might be better if problems within the college are first discussed and addressed within the college itself. [REDACTED_NAME] When negative posts are shared publicly—whether about professors, students, or internal issues—it doesn’t just affect the people involved. [REDACTED_NAME]). It can be unfair for others to be associated with something they didn’t contribute to. If there are real problems, I truly hope they are given proper attention and resolved through the right channels. Not everything needs to be brought to the public, especially when the issue can still be talked about and settled among yourselves. Once something is posted online, it spreads quickly and can turn into a much bigger issue than intended. Chismis pathway with major changes pa yan. My piece of "Drought of Leadership", as one of those labelled illusions The piece “The Illusion of SG” speaks in a voice many students recognize—frustrated, disappointed, and yearning for a system that truly listens. Beneath its sharp tone lies not rebellion, but a desire to be heard, to be counted, and to matter. That longing deserves acknowledgment, not dismissal. There is a truth that cannot be ignored: the current Student Government was formed through appointment, not a general election. On paper, this undeniably weakens the ideal of representation. In a perfect democratic setting, leadership is legitimized by votes, campaigns, and consent. That principle stands. However, journalism also demands rationality—anchored in facts and grounded in present realities, not ideals alone. Critique must walk hand in hand with context. This column does not dwell on the failures or controversies of past administrations. What matters is the present. And the present reality is this: the formation of the current SG was not a conquest—it was a struggle. Leadership positions did not attract eager candidates. In fact, no one wanted to run. It took repeated encouragement, persuasion, and even forceful convincing before individuals agreed to take on responsibility—not out of ambition, but reluctance. Another often-omitted truth: before the previous administration ended, there were genuine discussions about holding an election. Two parties initially expressed interest. Circumstances, however, intervened. One party withdrew. The remaining group was incomplete and unprepared to field a full slate. What should have been a contest dissolved into uncertainty. Responsibility then fell on individuals who, by their own admission, did not feel ready. Yet readiness is not always the starting point of leadership—sometimes, it is its outcome. These students stood their ground not because they believed they were entitled, but because no one else stepped forward. The claim that “leaders never spoke to the students” also warrants closer scrutiny. The first General Assembly existed for that very purpose—to speak, to listen, to question, and to engage. If voices went unheard, we must ask honestly: were they absent, silent, or disengaged? Participation is not a one-way demand. Representation requires presence on both sides. This brings us to a deeper and more uncomfortable truth: leadership is becoming scarce. Not because opportunities are lacking, but because willingness is. If the system is flawed, where were the alternative parties? Where were the applications? Where were the students ready to fight for leadership rather than merely criticize its absence? Democracy does not collapse only when elections fail, it also weakens when citizens refuse to participate. So perhaps what we are facing is not merely an illusion of Student Government, but a drought of leadership: a season where ideals remain high, but volunteers are few; where many demand representation, yet few are willing to shoulder its weight. The challenge, then, is not to tear down those who reluctantly stepped forward, but to ask ourselves: if we believe so strongly in choice, accountability, and consent—are we ready to lead, or only to judge? Because governance—student or otherwise—cannot survive on critique alone. It requires courage, on both sides of the aisle.

The Illusion of SG—or a Drought of Leadership and Student Responsibility The recent column branding the Student Government (SG) as an “illusion” because of the absence of elections warrants a response—not out of hostility, but out of responsibility to context, truth, and fairness. Opinions are valid. They are the lifeblood of a free press. But opinions are not synonymous with facts. When stripped of context, they risk oversimplifying complex realities. To declare that a student government is illegitimate solely because it was appointed rather than elected is to examine the outcome while ignoring the circumstances that produced it. The assertion that “what we have is not student governance, but an administration distinguished as representation” is rhetorically striking, but logically incomplete. Governance is not defined exclusively by the method of selection; it is defined by function, accountability, and service. When students organize activities, represent student interests, manage programs, and respond to student needs, governance is taking place—regardless of whether leadership emerged through election or necessity. The more pressing question remains unaddressed: Why were there no elections in the first place? Was there a dictatorship in place? Administrative interference? A compromised Commission on Elections? Or was it, as records suggest, a sustained leadership vacuum within the student body itself? For several consecutive years, no general student election took place—not because students were denied the opportunity to run, but because no one chose to do so. These are not assumptions. These are facts. The last contested SG election occurred in , where two parties were presented and elected. In the years that followed, repeated calls for candidacy were made. On August 23, , a filing of candidacy was publicly announced—yet no student complied. Again, on July 28, , an election call for SY – was posted. Only a limited number of Certificates of Candidacy were submitted, insufficient even to form two competing partylists. The platform existed. The freedom was available. Participation did not materialize. To describe the resulting SG as merely an “administration disguised as representation” disregards the reality that representation cannot exist without participants willing to represent. When students decline to lead, appointment becomes not a power grab, but a mechanism of continuity. Without it, student services, organizations, and activities would simply cease. This framing also diminishes the efforts of the officers—past and present—who chose to serve when others would not. It overlooks the sleepless nights, unpaid labor, overtime meetings, and personal sacrifices made to sustain events, initiatives, and even the platforms that allow student publications to operate. It must also be acknowledged that the appointed SG introduced reforms: new projects, new events, and student-centered initiatives that many currently enjoy. These were not the products of an illusion, but of a government adapting to institutional realities and striving to remain relevant amid declining student participation. Criticism from a publication should be principled—but it must also be consistent. Before labeling student governance as an illusion, it is worth reflecting on parallel structures within student institutions. If legitimacy is to be measured strictly by elections and experience, then the same scrutiny must apply elsewhere. A publication whose staff is largely composed of individuals without prior journalism experience—reportedly comprising the majority of its positions—cannot reasonably claim exemption from that standard. Recruitment by necessity rather than qualification is not unique to student government; it is a shared condition of student-led institutions. This is not a dismissal of student voices, but a reminder of shared responsibility. If there is an illusion present, it is not governance—it is participation. Leadership does not begin at the ballot box. It begins with willingness. Until more students choose to step forward, to run, and to accept accountability, elections alone will not resolve what is clearly a deeper problem. This was not an illusion of position. It was—and remains—a crisis of leadership. And that crisis cannot be solved by critique alone. /

Kawawa naman kayong mga adings na first yr nursing students. So many of you have shined brightly during high school- being SSLG presidents, VPs, or officers; competing AND CONSISTENTLY WINNING in numerous competitions (major and minor); participating in a multitude of events; receiving many awards (kayo pa yung mga nakakakuha ng leadership awards); not to mention, you were all academic achievers. [REDACTED_NAME] what happened when you entered [REDACTED_NAME]? Not all of you are able to prove your worth, strengths, talents, and abilities (especially in leadership). I think one reason for this is due to the lack of events, activities, and programs in the Univ. Kung meron man, based naman sa availability in sched. And we know naman na minsan 7am to 7pm and mga adings (how are they supposed to join kase even if may vacant naman sila its being used to rest and review for upcoming quizzes or exams). Another reason, I believe, is the student environment. You've made nursing education extremely tiring na minsan tinatamad na sila to join in activities. In fact, its not just tiring, but also demanding- to the point that they need to stay up late to complete all activities given by other subs while also review for neverending quizzes and exams. Personally, I am not from [CAR]. However, I am speaking for my younger cousin as I see his struggles in BSN at this university. I know that he and his capabilities are far beyond everyone's expectations. Hindi [REDACTED_NAME], but for the abilities of his whole batch. Kudos to NARS for trying to engage every member of their college. I just wanna request that you provide more opportunities for the students' growth and for opportunities to showcase what they can actually do- not just developing programs intended solely for fun. I know the seniors may be busy, as I am a BSN graduate myself so I know how hard it is. But please take my suggestion into consideration. [REDACTED_NAME] abilities [REDACTED_NAME]. This is also a reminder for other organizations to do the same. Thank you for hearing me out

Hello, I just want to share some thoughts based on what I’ve heard from my co-students who represent our university. I know that there are many organizations in the university, but what really stood out to me was when they shared that they were not given any incentives, even though they were representing the school and, in a way, becoming the face of our university. They gave up a lot of their time, energy, and even personal priorities just to be able to represent the university. Juggling academics with trainings, practices, and events is not easy, yet they still chose to show up and give their best. Their efforts may seem simple to some, but they play a big role in building the university’s image and reputation. With that, I hope these students can at least be given a chance to receive incentives—not just for the sake of rewards, but to let them feel appreciated and motivated. Even small recognition can mean a lot, especially for students who continue to serve and represent the university with dedication and heart.

But the people who were actually in the trenches with her know that her friendship came with a hidden price tag. While she was playing the supportive bestie to your face, she was busy trading your secrets for social currency with the higher-ups. She didn't just climb the ladder; she used her friends as the rungs. To the batch, she’s a success story. To those who were actually there, she’s the ultimate cautionary tale of a backstabber in a blazer. To the people who defended her, goodluck.

📢 Dear Single Fathers of [CAR], We Would Love to Hear From You! 📢 Greetings! We are fourth-year Psychology students, and in partial fulfillment of the requirements for the Bachelor of Science in Psychology, we are currently conducting research titled: 👨‍👧‍👦 PSYCHOLOGICAL WELL-BEING AND COPING STRATEGIES OF SINGLE FATHERS 👨‍👧‍👦 This study aims to explore the psychological well-being, coping strategies, and lived experiences of single fathers as they navigate the responsibilities, challenges, and realities of raising a family on their own. We are inviting single father participants who: ✅ Are 18 years old and above ✅ Are currently residing in [CAR] ✅ Are actively raising one or more children ✅ Are willing to share their experiences Know someone who fits the criteria? Please feel free to tag them, share this post, or send us a message. By participating in this study and sharing your experiences, you are helping us better understand the mental health and coping experiences of single fathers. Your participation will greatly contribute to meaningful psychological research. For more information and inquiries, you may message or contact: [REDACTED_NAME] 📞 📧 [REDACTED_NAME] This study is conducted in collaboration with the [CAR] MSWDO, [CAR] [DEPARTMENT]– Psychology Department, and the Local Government and Non-Government Units. For pre-registration, kindly click and answer the Google Form link below! Rest assured that all information will be kept strictly confidential and will be used for academic purposes only. Thank you for your time, support, and for sharing your story. 💙

It’s late and for some reason I can’t sleep. I was playing a random playlist, and the first song that came up was [REDACTED_NAME] by [REDACTED_NAME]. Just like that, I was transported back to a memory I hadn’t thought about in years. It was my cousin’s birthday. The house was full of laughter, chatter, and the smell of cake and food in the air. At one point, there was a dance portion where the host randomly paired every girl with a boy. By chance, he was paired with me. We were both shy, the kind of quiet that makes you want to disappear into the background. I assumed we’d just laugh awkwardly and go back to our seats, but instead, he reintroduced himself. He noticed my hesitation about stepping onto the dance floor and made a light joke about having two left feet. He told me we didn’t have to dance if I didn’t want to, then gently took my hand and led me back to my seat, sitting quietly beside me. There was no rush, no pressure, just a quiet, kind presence that made me feel safe. As the dance floor filled with hesitant pairs, he asked again if I’d changed my mind. This time I relented. He offered his hand again and led me onto the floor. I honestly didn’t feel very pretty that night. My dress wasn’t my best, my hair and makeup felt rushed and messy, but somehow, he made me feel like the most beautiful person in the room. The dance went on slowly, gently. He guided me with care, told me when he would spin me, and stayed completely gentlemanly the whole time. We made small talk as we moved together, and even when the host instructed everyone to switch partners now and then, he stayed with me. When the music paused for a small Q&A, everyone else let go of their partner’s hands. We didn’t. He held on, and the warmth of his hand made me not want to let go either. We only separated when it was time to switch with the birthday celebrant. After the dance, I meant to find him and thank him for the wonderful night, but I had to help my sis, and when I returned, he was gone. For some reason, his name just slipped my mind. And when I realized my sis might have liked him too, I chose quietly to let the memory stay with me and didn't bother looking for him anymore. I don’t know if this will ever reach you, Mr. [REDACTED_NAME], but I want to say thank you. That small, effortless kindness you showed me that night quietly set my standard for men. Because of that, I eventually found a good one of my own. And even though I never got to know you properly, I like to think you found your own person too. Somewhere out there, I hope they make you feel seen, cherished, and a little bit enchanted just like you made me feel that night.

This is about clarity. About integrity. About what it means to train as a nurse. We were told that [REDACTED_NAME] (CA 1) is not a prerequisite. And yet, here comes Competency Appraisal 2 (CA 2) being offered this second semester. Think about that for a moment. How are students expected to progress in competencies if the foundation hasn’t been properly met? How can the next step assume mastery when the first step is officially postponed? This isn’t a question of compassion or flexibility — this is a question of standards. Every decision made at the top sends a message: That rules can bend when convenient. That progression can happen without proper preparation. That principles can be traded for expediency. We are future nurses. Every compromise now sets a precedent. Every unclear policy now teaches us that competency is negotiable, standards are optional, and accountability is flexible. This is not about failure. This is about how decisions are being made. About the lack of transparency and rationale in a system that is supposed to safeguard both learning and patient safety. If you are making these decisions, remember: Students aren’t just numbers. Competencies aren’t just boxes to tick. Every deviation from standards matters. We can support students struggling to meet requirements without undermining what those requirements are meant to ensure. We can be compassionate without erasing the rules that protect integrity. Clarity is not cruelty. Integrity is not inconvenience. Standards are not optional. If CA 1 is truly not a prerequisite, then explain how CA 2 can logically exist this semester. Don’t hide behind convenience. Don’t pretend ambiguity is guidance. We need answers. Not platitudes. Not postponements. Not “adjustments.” This is the moment to show that principles still matter in [CAR] [DEPARTMENT].

Now that the semester is over and the grades are out, I’m done staying polite about something that has clearly failed many students—a certain kind of professor. Let’s stop pretending this is about “student weakness” or “lack of effort.” Some of you simply do not want to teach. You show up, rush through slides, avoid questions, and call it a day—as if your presence alone is enough to justify your salary and your title. It isn’t. From the first day, it already felt like we were expected to fend for ourselves. No guidance, no patience, no real effort to make sure students actually understood the material. Just speed, detachment, and the unspoken message: “Keep up or get left behind.” That is not education—that is abandonment disguised as rigor. And then comes the audacity to question low student evaluations. Let’s be clear: evaluations are not acts of rebellion. They are consequences. They are students finally being given a voice after an entire semester of being ignored. If dozens of students say the same thing, the problem is no longer “sensitivity”—it’s you. Not all students can survive on self-study alone. That is not a flaw; that is reality. Education is supposed to meet students where they are, not punish them for learning differently. Teaching at breakneck speed, refusing to slow down, and brushing off confusion is not “challenging students.” It is laziness dressed up as toughness. Yes, my grade is low. And yes, I acknowledge my part in that. But don’t insult our intelligence by acting like teaching quality has no impact on student outcomes. A subject becomes unbearable not because it is hard, but because it is taught by someone who has stripped it of meaning. Motivation dies when effort is one-sided. What makes this worse is the hypocrisy. This State U proudly preach “quality education,” yet tolerate professors who clearly have no passion, no empathy, and no accountability. Where exactly is the quality when students feel anxious asking questions? When clarification feels like an inconvenience? When learning becomes a solo struggle instead of a shared process? The title “professor” is not a shield from criticism. It is not a free pass to disengage. If teaching feels like a chore, if students feel like burdens, and if explaining concepts feels beneath you—then maybe you are in the wrong profession. Because real educators don’t just deliver information; they make sure it is understood. This is a general statement, but it comes from a very real experience with a very real type of teacher. And if this feels personal to you—good. Because discomfort is often the first step toward reflection. If you feel attacked, maybe it’s because you recognize yourself in these words. Students are not asking for perfection. We are asking for effort, clarity, and basic respect. Anything less is a failure not of students—but of the system that allows this kind of teaching to continue unchecked.
