"CHEATERS NEVER WIN" How true is it? Cheaters seem like they're the ones winning. In every quiz and exam, the honest ones are always "lugi" when the cheaters don't get caught. they say "study smarter, not harder", but some people could have taken this out of context and made it their reason to cheat. they are praised for being "smart" but does that mean the honest ones are stupid, because of — well because they were honest? a little silip sa notes, a little silip sa katabi — a little cheating? cheating is cheating. One thing about it is, cheating is simply dishonesty, and dishonesty breeds corruption. They go hand in hand. You cannot be corrupt without being dishonest. So para sa mga kaklase kong [REDACTED_NAME], ano na? [REDACTED_NAME] maysa kayo tun idjy contractors [REDACTED_NAME] It's also rather ironic how we condemn those who are seated in power for their corruption, and rally against them, but fail to realize that OUR CLASSROOMS ARE BECOMING THE BREEDING GROUND OF CORRUPTION — a bold statement, but somehow there is also truth in it. But how true could the quote, "cheaters never win", be? what happens to cheaters when they graduate from uni? — a proposition: that many of those who cheated in uni and graduated, don't pass the board exam while those who pass are usually the ones who are dishonest or corrupt in their work. "kasanu [REDACTED_NAME]?", one might ask. when cheating or dishonesty has already become part of the lifestyle of a person, you cannot say that they could work without it "corruption is because of greed tho" —well, greed for more points? parang same "but we'll fail when we don't cheat" — no, you'll fail when you don't study. we all get desperate when we have nothing but when you studied, it shouldn't be the case. stop making excuses even if cheaters do "win", they always "lose" in some way or another. i remember one of my instructors put and i quote, "FOR CHEATERS: hell is full of people like you", at the bottom of the test page. i mean, you're like trading your soul for more points when you cheat. Winning or losing, whichever, it may seem, the fruit doesn't grow immediately after planting the seed. We all sow, we all reap in the right season. Let those who were honest ngayong 1st sem rest with this wisdom in mind. —from those na na-"lugi"

Even mature African tulip trees in the Cordillera remain a serious ecological and safety concern. [REDACTED_NAME] is listed among the invasive alien plants in the Philippines under the National Invasive Species Strategy and Action Plan – (NISSAP) of the DENR, which identifies it as capable of rapidly invading disturbed and natural forests and forming dense thickets that outcompete native vegetation. Its ability to produce masses of wind-dispersed seeds and resprout after cutting allows continuous spread even from a few individuals. Moreover, its wood is soft and brittle, making its mature stems and branches prone to breakage and hazardous during storms—posing risk to people, infrastructure, and surrounding forest. Given these traits—persistent invasiveness, competitive dominance, and structural instability—removing old African tulip trees remains justified to protect native biodiversity, forest regeneration, and human safety.

I just want to express my curiosity and opinion on what led the administration to cut down 151 African tulip trees. Despite the stated reason that these trees pose threats to people and infrastructure, I find the explanation vague. Firstly, most of these trees are planted near the university fences, and there are other trees that are even closer to college buildings and students. Why were those not included? Why specifically target African tulip trees? Secondly, these trees have been part of the university for so long, and now the administration plans to remove a significant number of them. Why the sudden decision to cut them down? Is this because of previous typhoons? Thirdly, in my opinion, cutting down these 151 trees will negatively affect the existing ecosystem and the daily life of students and employees. The impact may not be felt immediately, but we can never be certain unless it is scientifically proven. Overall, I find this large-scale tree cutting troubling, even if it has a legal permit. It makes me question why and how these trees pose such a significant threat to people and infrastructure. The poster released by the administration does not explain this action in enough detail. This is not meant to throw shade at the current administration but the removal of these trees is concerning for people like me who care about the environment especially now that we are facing rising temperatures and harsher weather conditions. We need these trees more than ever. I hope this post encourages students to be curious and to ask questions about the administration’s decisions with the purpose of not to bring them down, rather help improve and find better solutions for the needs and challenges of our university. I am done putting my life and the life of my people into the hands of those who don’t give a damn. Into the hands of people incapable of justice, incapable of protecting our lands. So rise up. They are meant to serve us, to guide us to prosperity, not drag us into the dirt. People are dying because of their incompetence. And if we don’t act now, we could be next. This is not a mistake. This is a warning.

During my time serving as a student leader, I did all my duties and responsibilities within and beyond my understanding. Not for people's validation, not for the fulfillment they say you will feel when helping people but it was my WILL and strong sense of servanthood. But yet as my term proceeded, why did it felt as a recurring burden of serving selfish, incompetent, ingrate people at my very own expense. Gave all what i had, my time, my effort, my very own being. While deliberating with myself, maybe I am in the wrong environment, maybe it is just accumulation of unfortunate events, maybe I am burnt out, or maybe just exhausted entirely. Having that wonderful rest which i am grateful for allowing myself. But then, why does it hurt serving people? Was my approach wrong? Making them reliable to me rather than teaching them to stand on there own? Or maybe the greater population is just bunch of insufferable people with no respect and consideration, draining and taking advantage of your selfless service? Or maybe i gave too much or gave myself entirely that i do not even know who am i anymore? Or maybe... I am not up for this position. Rotting to my very own incompetence, degrading to my own suffering and pain... For this, I conclude my very respectable position to vacancy and resignation to save myself.

As a student, it’s frustrating to see how the school seems so focused on money. They use fees and payments as constant excuses, yet the support we actually receive whether financial or physical often feels lacking. It creates this impression that money matters more than the students’ real needs. On a different point, it’s also disheartening how the school suddenly steps in only when a student achieves something. They claim credit for accomplishments they didn’t genuinely support, appearing only when the hard work is already done. Meanwhile, the students who struggled and pushed themselves toward those goals rarely receive the same recognition they deserve.
